draft.introduction: Odyssey 23 narrates the recognition scene between Odysseus and Penelope, testing each other’s identity through the secret of their marriage bed. It is the emotional climax of the entire poem and the archetype of homecoming in Western literature.

translation.text: Penelope said, ‘Come then, Eurycleia, move the bed outside the chamber.’ And Odysseus, troubled, said: ‘Woman, who has moved my bed? For I built it myself around a living olive tree, and made it the bedpost.’ Then Penelope’s knees grew weak, for she recognized the sure signs. She wept and ran to him and threw her arms about his neck.
translation.original_text: τῆς δ’ αὐτοῦ λύτο γούνατα καὶ φίλον ἦτορ

translator_notes.note: The immovable bed, built around a living olive tree, is the symbol of the marriage’s rootedness. Penelope’s test matches Odysseus’ cunning with her own—she is his true equal in metis. The recognition is mutual, not one-sided.
